[music] &gt;&gt; Good evening. This is Daily News from Addis Standard broadcasting from Addis Ababa. Today is Tuesday, April 7th, 2026. Here are the top stories we're following tonight. UN envoy meets RSF leader in Nairobi for Sudan de-escalation talks. The United Nations envoy for Sudan, Pekka Haavisto, has met Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo in Nairobi to discuss steps toward de-escalating the conflict in Sudan. The meeting is part of Haavisto's regional tour and follows earlier talks in Khartoum with Abdel Fattah al-Burhan. UN spokesperson Stephane Dujarric described the discussions as a constructive opportunity focused on civilian protection, noting that stakeholders have expressed willingness to cooperate. Haavisto also held consultations in Cairo with regional actors and Sudanese UN scales up humanitarian operations with coordinator Denise Brown returning to Khartoum. The effort signals renewed international engagement to ease Sudan's prolonged conflict. EU forces secure release of hijacked Iranian vessel off Somalia. European Union naval forces under Operation Atalanta have secured the release of an Iranian-flagged vessel hijacked off the coast of Somalia. The fishing dow Al Waseemi was seized about 400 nautical miles east of Mogadishu on March 24th. According to Operation Atalanta, the vessel was freed after pirates abandoned it under pressure from coordinated EU naval and air operations conducted alongside Somali maritime police. The mission said the operation prioritized the safety of those on board using sustained surveillance and strategic positioning to force the hijackers to withdraw. The vessel was ultimately released on April 5th near Somalia's northwestern coast. The development highlights ongoing maritime security efforts in the region amid persistent piracy threats. Tigray interim president meets PM Abiy on final day of mandate. On the final day of his mandate, Tadesse Worede met Abiy Ahmed to present his annual report, according to a statement from the Prime Minister's office. [music] The brief statement said discussions focused on the report and its findings without disclosing further details. Tadesse's tenure began on April 8th, 2025, when he was appointed to lead the interim administration in Tigray region, replacing Getachew Reda. His mandate included overseeing the return of internally displaced persons and advancing disarmament and reintegration efforts under the Pretoria Agreement. The meeting marks the conclusion of a one-year term described by federal authorities as part of a broader leadership transition process. [music] The development comes amid ongoing political and recovery efforts in the region. Ethiopia mourns trailblazing diplomat Ambassador Konjit SineGiorgis. Konjit SineGiorgis, one of Ethiopia's most distinguished and longest-serving diplomats, has passed away at the age of 86. Born in Harar in 1940, she began her career at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in the early 1960s, rising through the ranks to become Ethiopia's second female ambassador after Yodit Imiru. Over more than five decades, she served in key diplomatic posts in Ottawa, Cairo, Tel Aviv, and Vienna, &gt;&gt; [music] &gt;&gt; and represented Ethiopia at the African Union and the United Nations Economic Commission for Africa. She also contributed to regional peace efforts as a special advisor during the IGAD-led South Sudan peace process. [music] Ambassador Konjit's career spanned multiple political eras, beginning during the reign of Haile Selassie I. Widely regarded as a pioneer for women in diplomacy, she's remembered for her resilience, service, and lasting impact on Ethiopia's foreign relations. Fuel shortages linked to Iran war strain Ethiopia, may ease regional tensions. Global fuel disruptions tied to the conflict involving Iran are impacting Ethiopia, potentially limiting the risk of wider conflict in the Horn of Africa. An analysis by Barron's indicates that supply shocks linked to the Strait of Hormuz have led to fuel shortages in Ethiopia, prompting rationing and affecting key sectors. The report says reduced diesel and jet fuel supplies have constrained military logistics as authorities balance operational needs with maintaining services such as Ethiopian Airlines. The developments come amid earlier tensions between Ethiopia and Eritrea, which had raised fears of renewed conflict. While troop movements and clashes had fueled concerns of escalation, analysts suggest the fuel crisis may have limited the capacity for sustained military operations. The situation underscores how external geopolitical shocks are shaping dynamics in the Horn, with resource constraints potentially contributing to a fragile pause in tensions. Police charge trafficking kingpin linked to deaths, abuse of migrants. Ethiopian federal police say they have referred to prosecutors the case of suspected trafficking kingpin Yitbarek Gebre and nine accomplices accused of running a network linked to over 3,000 victims. Authorities allege the operation, active since 2018, was responsible for more than 100 deaths and the abuse of dozens of women. The suspect was arrested in Shire following a coordinated operation supported by the Khartoum process. Police say the network transported migrants to Libya, where victims were reportedly detained, tortured, and forced to pay ransom. Investigators identified multiple aliases used by the suspect and tracked financial transactions exceeding 3 billion birr. Authorities say assets linked to the network have been frozen, with investigations supported by national and international partners. The case highlights ongoing efforts to combat human trafficking and cross-border exploitation. That's all for tonight's Daily News. Thank you for watching.
